title: Observable Characteristics
data_note: Quantitative benchmarks for observed UAP performance characteristics compared to known aerospace, physics, and engineering limits. Values are drawn from declassified military reports, peer-reviewed physics, and publicly available aerospace data.

statistics.source: Knuth, K. H., Powell, R. M., & Reali, P. A. (2019). Estimating Flight Characteristics of Anomalous Unidentified Aerial Vehicles. Entropy, 21(10), 939. https://doi.org/10.3390/e21100939
statistics.context: Estimated from USS Princeton SPY-1 radar tracking during the 2004 Nimitz Carrier Strike Group encounters. Object reportedly dropped from 28,000 ft to sea level in 0.78 seconds.
statistics.verification_note: Derived from radar data analysis by Knuth et al. Raw radar data remains classified. The 5,300 g figure is an upper-bound estimate assuming instantaneous acceleration profiles.

statistics.metric: Human sustained g-tolerance with anti-g equipment
statistics.source: Whinnery, J. E. & Forster, E. M. (2013). The +Gz-induced loss of consciousness curve. Extreme Physiology & Medicine, 2, 19. https://doi.org/10.1186/2046-7648-2-19
statistics.context: Maximum sustained acceleration tolerated by trained fighter pilots wearing anti-g suits (AGSM technique). Sustained for several seconds before G-induced loss of consciousness (GLOC).
statistics.verification_note: Well-established in aerospace medicine literature. Centrifuge studies at NASC Warminster and USAF School of Aerospace Medicine confirm this range.

statistics.source: Stapp, J. P. (1951). Human exposures to linear deceleration. Air Force Aero Medical Laboratory Report. See also: Ryan, D. J. (2019). John Paul Stapp and human tolerance to deceleration. Aviation, Space, and Environmental Medicine, 90(9), 816-818.
statistics.context: Col. John Paul Stapp survived 46.2 g peak deceleration on a rocket sled in 1954. Approximately 20 g is considered a survivable brief-exposure threshold for trained individuals with restraint systems.
statistics.verification_note: Based on Holloman Air Force Base rocket sled tests (1947-1958). The 46.2 g peak survived by Stapp was a fraction-of-a-second impulse; ~20 g is the practical brief-duration limit.

statistics.source: Wade, M. (2019). HIBEX. Encyclopedia Astronautica. http://www.astronautix.com/h/hibex.html; See also: Raytheon/DARPA historical ABM program references.
statistics.context: Sprint-class anti-ballistic missile interceptor (High-G Boost Experiment) developed in the 1960s. Achieved ~400 g during boost phase, one of the highest sustained accelerations for a guided projectile.
statistics.verification_note: Documented in declassified ABM program records. The Sprint missile (operational successor) achieved ~100 g. HIBEX experimental tests reached higher values. Exact figures vary by source (300-400 g range).

statistics.source: Touboul, P. et al. (2022). MICROSCOPE Mission: Final Results of the Test of the Equivalence Principle. Physical Review Letters, 129, 121102. https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.129.121102
statistics.context: The MICROSCOPE satellite mission tested the Weak Equivalence Principle to an unprecedented precision of ~1e-15 (Eötvös parameter eta), finding no violation. This is 100x improvement over prior ground-based torsion balance experiments.
statistics.verification_note: Published in Physical Review Letters. CNES/ONERA mission. Result: eta = [-1.5 +/- 2.3(stat) +/- 1.5(syst)] x 10^-15, consistent with zero violation.

statistics.metric: Speed of sound at sea level (ISA conditions)
statistics.source: International Standard Atmosphere (ISA), ICAO Doc 7488/3. See also: NIST Reference on Constants. https://www.nist.gov/
statistics.context: Speed of sound in dry air at 20 degrees C and standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa). Commonly used as the reference for Mach number calculations in UAP speed estimates.
statistics.verification_note: Standard physical constant. Exact value depends on temperature and humidity; 343 m/s at 20 C is the standard textbook value.

statistics.metric: Space Shuttle reentry surface temperature at Mach 25
statistics.source: NASA. (2006). Space Shuttle Orbiter Thermal Protection System. NASA Facts FS-2006-07-62-MSFC. https://www.nasa.gov/centers/kennedy/about/information/shuttle_reference.html
statistics.context: Maximum temperature on the leading edges and nose cap of the Space Shuttle during reentry at approximately Mach 25 (7.8 km/s). Reinforced carbon-carbon (RCC) panels experienced up to ~1650 C.
statistics.verification_note: Well-documented by NASA. The TPS design specification used 1650 C for RCC panels; actual measured temperatures during STS missions confirmed this range. Some sources cite up to 1700 C on specific surfaces.

statistics.source: Sweetman, B. (2009). Lockheed Stealth. Zenith Press. See also: Aronstein, D. C. & Piccirillo, A. C. (1997). Have Blue and the F-117A: Evolution of the Stealth Fighter. AIAA.
statistics.context: The F-117 was the first operational stealth aircraft. Its radar cross-section (RCS) is estimated at roughly the equivalent of a marble to a small ball bearing at certain frequencies and aspects, approximately 0.001 to 0.01 m^2.
statistics.verification_note: Exact RCS values remain classified. Published estimates range from 0.001 to 0.01 m^2 depending on frequency, aspect angle, and source. The commonly cited figure is roughly 0.003 m^2.

statistics.source: Dyson, F. W., Eddington, A. S., & Davidson, C. (1920). A Determination of the Deflection of Light by the Sun's Gravitational Field, from Observations Made at the Total Eclipse of May 29, 1919. Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society A, 220, 291-333. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsta.1920.0009
statistics.context: General relativity predicts light passing the Sun's limb is deflected by 1.75 arcseconds, double the Newtonian prediction. First confirmed by Eddington's 1919 solar eclipse expedition. Modern VLBI measurements confirm GR prediction to ~0.01% accuracy.
statistics.verification_note: Foundational result of general relativity. Modern confirmation: Shapiro et al. (2004) using VLBI achieved 0.02% agreement with GR. The 1.75 arcsecond value is exact for a grazing ray.

statistics.metric: Water density ratio to air at sea level
statistics.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 105th Edition. Water density ~998 kg/m^3; air density ~1.204 kg/m^3 at 20 C, 1 atm.
statistics.context: Water is approximately 830 times denser than air at sea-level conditions. This enormous density ratio makes trans-medium travel (air-to-water or water-to-air) extremely challenging for conventional vehicles due to sudden changes in drag, buoyancy, and structural loads.
statistics.verification_note: Standard physical property ratio. Exact value depends on temperature and salinity; 998/1.204 ~ 829. Rounded to ~830x as commonly cited.

statistics.metric: Dynamic pressure in water at 100 m/s
statistics.source: Fundamental fluid dynamics: q = 0.5 * rho * v^2. With rho_water = 998 kg/m^3 and v = 100 m/s. See also: Munson, B. R. et al. (2012). Fundamentals of Fluid Mechanics, 7th Ed., Wiley.
statistics.context: Dynamic pressure q = 0.5 * rho * v^2 = 0.5 * 998 * 100^2 = 4.99 MPa. This extreme hydrodynamic pressure at relatively modest speeds illustrates why high-speed underwater travel requires extraordinary structural and propulsion solutions.
statistics.verification_note: Direct calculation from Bernoulli's equation. The ~5 MPa dynamic pressure at 100 m/s in water is equivalent to ~50 atmospheres, comparable to the stagnation pressure on a re-entry vehicle at Mach 6 in air.

statistics.source: Jane's Underwater Warfare Systems; See also: Cowan, R. (2005). Riding a Bubble. New Scientist, 186(2500), 32-35. Soviet/Russian supercavitating torpedo program documentation.
statistics.context: The Russian VA-111 Shkval supercavitating torpedo achieves speeds of approximately 200 knots (~370 km/h or ~100 m/s) by generating a gas bubble envelope (supercavitation) that dramatically reduces skin friction drag. Conventional torpedoes are limited to ~50-60 knots.
statistics.verification_note: Widely reported as ~200 knots (some sources cite 200+). Entered Soviet Navy service in 1977. Exact performance specifications remain classified. The ~200 knot figure is the consensus estimate from multiple open sources.

statistics.unit: g (depending on assumed descent time 0.78–2.0 s)
statistics.context: Sensitivity analysis on the canonical 5,300 g estimate. At 0.78 s: ~5,300 g; at 1.0 s: ~3,200 g; at 1.5 s: ~1,420 g; at 2.0 s: ~800 g. Even the most conservative estimate exceeds HIBEX (~400 g) by 2×.
statistics.verification_note: Derived from Knuth et al. 2019 radar data analysis. The range reflects uncertainty in the assumed descent time window. All values exceed engineered sustained acceleration limits.
